ann-001.title: The Layered Retelling
ann-001.content: This dialogue is not a stenographic record. It reaches us through a chain of retelling: Aristodemus, then Apollodorus, then Glaucon.

ann-002.title: Rules for Drinking and Speaking
ann-002.content: The party first agrees to drink lightly and dismiss the flute-girl, then turns the evening into a round of speeches praising Eros.

ann-003.title: Love and Honor
ann-003.content: Phaedrus links love with shame, honor, and sacrifice, stressing its ethical force in politics and war.

ann-004.title: The Two Aphrodites
ann-004.content: Pausanias distinguishes two kinds of love through the contrast between a heavenly and a common Aphrodite, and uses that distinction to build an ethical hierarchy.

ann-005.title: The Myth of the Round Humans
ann-005.content: Aristophanes uses myth to describe human beings as originally whole and later split apart, making love a quest to recover wholeness.

ann-006.title: Love Is Desire for What One Lacks
ann-006.content: Through Socratic questioning, Agathon's hymn becomes a conceptual analysis: love always reaches toward what it does not possess.

ann-007.title: Love as a Daimon
ann-007.content: Diotima defines love as a daimon, a being between gods and human beings, rather than a fully self-sufficient god.

ann-008.title: Procreation and Immortality
ann-008.content: The pursuit of immortality is realized through bodily reproduction and through the soul's creative productions, so love becomes tied to generation and transmission.

ann-009.title: The Ladder of Love
ann-009.content: From one beautiful body to all beautiful bodies, then to souls, institutions, and knowledge, the ascent culminates in Beauty itself.

ann-010.title: "Beauty Itself"
ann-010.content: Here "Beauty itself" means the eternal and unchanging reality of beauty, not the sum of beautiful things.

ann-011.title: The Silenus and Marsyas Image
ann-011.content: Alcibiades uses the opening Silenus statues and the flute-player Marsyas to describe Socrates as coarse on the outside and profound within.

ann-012.title: The Unity of Comedy and Tragedy
ann-012.content: The dawn debate at the end points toward an important judgment: the true poet should understand both comedy and tragedy.

ann-013.title: Agathon's Victory Banquet
ann-013.content: The banquet takes place after Agathon's first tragic victory, giving the whole discussion both literary prestige and the social aura of a rising young celebrity.

ann-014.title: Socrates Unusually Dressed Up
ann-014.content: Aristodemus pointedly notes that Socrates has bathed, put on shoes, and dressed carefully. The detail highlights his usual austerity and adds a small theatrical contrast to the visit.

ann-015.title: The Joke About Arriving Uninvited
ann-015.content: This scene plays with a proverb about going uninvited to a good man's feast. Socrates bends etiquette into a joke, suggesting that his relation to Agathon is not entirely formal.

ann-016.title: The Trance at the Porch
ann-016.content: Socrates stops outside and falls into thought while his companion enters first. The moment reinforces a recurring image of him as someone who can suddenly detach from ordinary social time.

ann-017.title: Discussion Begins After Libation
ann-017.content: The guests pour libations and sing a hymn before turning to conversation. Philosophy here grows out of symposium ritual rather than replacing it.

ann-018.title: The Flute-Girl Is Dismissed
ann-018.content: Greek symposia often included entertainers, musicians, and other forms of performance. Here the flute-girl is sent away so the evening can become a more orderly contest of speeches.

ann-019.title: The Physician Becomes Moderator
ann-019.content: Eryximachus does not only speak about medicine; he also regulates the pace of drinking. Bodily management, restraint, and conversational order are folded into the same medical logic.

ann-020.title: Sacrifice as Proof of Love's Power
ann-020.content: Phaedrus does not begin with definition but with examples such as Alcestis and Achilles. The encomiastic mode relies on heroic cases to show love as a force that can motivate self-sacrifice.

ann-021.title: Civic Law Enters the Question of Love
ann-021.content: Pausanias refuses to treat love as a purely private passion. Whether love is noble depends in part on how cities, laws, and customs give it shape.

ann-022.title: The Educational Frame of 'Noble' Love
ann-022.content: For Pausanias, a young man may accept pursuit without shame when the aim is virtue, wisdom, and long-term formation. The issue is not desire alone but the end toward which it is directed.

ann-023.title: Love Expanded into a Medical Principle
ann-023.content: Eryximachus extends Pausanias's distinction into the body itself: health comes from the right ordering of opposed elements, so love becomes a principle for organizing living balance.

ann-024.title: Seasons, Ritual, and Cosmic Order
ann-024.content: In Eryximachus's speech, love governs not only people but the seasons, cultivation, ritual, and the traffic between gods and mortals. Desire is rewritten as an almost cosmological principle of harmony.

ann-025.title: A Mythic Change of Register
ann-025.content: Aristophanes openly says he will not speak in the same way as the earlier speakers. The dialogue thus places medicine, law, poetry, and myth side by side as rival ways of explaining love.

ann-026.title: "The Other Half" Is Not Just a Soulmate
ann-026.content: In the round-human myth, the other half first explains the pain of division. What the lover seeks is not merely a predestined partner but a recovery of lost wholeness.

ann-027.title: Praise the God Before the Gifts
ann-027.content: Agathon argues that a proper encomium should first describe what the god is before listing the benefits he gives. It is a highly self-aware attempt to discipline the rhetoric of praise.

ann-028.title: The High Point of Ornate Encomium
ann-028.content: Agathon presents love as the youngest, softest, and happiest of gods in deliberately polished language. The speech's beauty is precisely what leaves room for Socrates to interrogate it.

ann-029.title: Applause Before Philosophical Scrutiny
ann-029.content: Aristodemus records the burst of applause. Plato lets rhetoric win socially before philosophy asks whether it is actually true.

ann-030.title: From Encomium to Examination
ann-030.content: Socrates does not answer with an even finer speech. He turns instead to questioning, shifting the dialogue from performative praise to dialectical testing.

ann-031.title: The Lover Does Not Possess What He Loves
ann-031.content: If love loves beauty and goodness, then the lover cannot already possess them in the same full sense. Socrates flips 'Eros is most beautiful' into the claim that love itself implies lack.

ann-032.title: "Daimon" Is Not a Modern Demon
ann-032.content: Here daimon means an in-between spiritual being, messenger, or mediator, not a later Christian-style demon. Diotima uses the term to place love between gods and humans, knowledge and ignorance.

ann-033.title: Child of Poverty and Resource
ann-033.content: Love is born from Poverty and Resource, so it is neither wealthy nor helpless. It lacks, yet it is also clever, scheming, and able to pursue what it wants.

ann-034.title: The Soul Can Also Give Birth
ann-034.content: Diotima treats poetry, law, education, and the creation of virtue as forms of spiritual reproduction. The search for immortality therefore extends beyond bloodline into cultural transmission.

ann-035.title: Philosophy Cast as an Initiatory Ascent
ann-035.content: Diotima divides the education of love into lower and higher stages, using language close to religious initiation. Plato presents philosophy as a discipline of gradual revelation.

ann-036.title: "Beauty" Also Means Nobility
ann-036.content: In Symposium, the beautiful often shades into what is admirable, noble, and worthy. The ascent toward Beauty itself therefore remains ethical and intellectual, not merely aesthetic.

ann-037.title: Alcibiades Interrupts the Ascent
ann-037.content: Immediately after the abstract climax of Beauty itself, the drunken Alcibiades bursts in and drags the discussion back toward bodies, desire, jealousy, and social theater.

ann-038.title: A Scene of Jealousy with Wreaths
ann-038.content: Alcibiades crowns Agathon and complains about Socrates at the same time. The symposium wreath suddenly becomes a prop in a public display of jealousy.

ann-039.title: Praising Socrates Instead of Eros
ann-039.content: Alcibiades formally departs from the assigned theme: he no longer defines love but bears witness to Socrates. The deviation also works like a counterexample, since attraction to Socrates does not automatically equal ascent to Beauty itself.

ann-040.title: Battlefield Testimony Replaces Definition
ann-040.content: The scenes at Potidaea and in retreat make Socrates' restraint and courage concrete. Here virtue is no longer argued for abstractly but supported by eyewitness testimony.

ann-041.title: Seating as Relationship Politics
ann-041.content: Agathon, Socrates, and Alcibiades keep shifting seats and contesting proximity. Physical placement becomes a visible map of intimacy, exclusion, and rivalry.

ann-042.title: From Ordered Symposium to Chaotic Drinking
ann-042.content: At the start the guests negotiate rules for drinking; at the end intruders collapse the whole event into disorderly revelry. The dialogue deliberately juxtaposes moderated symposium with uncontrolled intoxication.

ann-043.title: Glaucon's Question Opens the Outer Frame
ann-043.content: Glaucon's request is not casual small talk. It reopens a banquet long past and puts the questions of who asks, who remembers, and who retells directly in front of the reader.

ann-044.title: Temporal Distance Makes the Dialogue a Memory Experiment
ann-044.content: Apollodorus first corrects the date of the banquet, reminding us that this is not recent conversation but an old exchange preserved and reshaped by memory.

ann-045.title: Apollodorus's Philosophical Zeal
ann-045.content: He treats philosophical conversation as the one truly joyful and useful activity, while money and ordinary business strike him as empty. That almost obsessive tone helps explain why others view him as half-mad.

ann-046.title: A Homeric Joke About Going to Dinner
ann-046.content: Aristodemus invokes a Homeric-style scene to excuse his arrival without an invitation. The allusion is lightly self-mocking rather than a serious confession of bad manners.

ann-047.title: Agathon Wants to 'Share' Wisdom
ann-047.content: Agathon treats Socrates' pause in the porch as if it had just yielded some precious discovery and wants to sit close enough to partake in it. The line sets up a joke about whether wisdom can really be shared that way.

ann-048.title: Wisdom Does Not Flow Like Water
ann-048.content: Socrates responds with the image of water running through a thread from one cup into another. The joke grants our wish for transferable wisdom while quietly suggesting that genuine thought cannot simply be poured over.

ann-049.title: The Hangover Makes Light Drinking Plausible
ann-049.content: The guests openly admit that they drank heavily the night before, which makes the later decision to drink lightly feel practical rather than idealized. Philosophy begins here amid bodily fatigue.

ann-050.title: Why Phaedrus Speaks First
ann-050.content: Phaedrus sits first and is also treated as the one who raised the theme, so he naturally begins. Order of speaking is therefore tied to thematic ownership as well as seating.

ann-051.title: The Contradictory Zone of Erotic Custom
ann-051.content: Pausanias stresses that the same city can both encourage and police erotic attachments. Greek love is thus embedded in family honor, civic discipline, and education rather than left as a purely private matter.

ann-052.title: The Hiccup Pulls High Speech Back to the Body
ann-052.content: Just as Aristophanes is about to speak, he is interrupted by a fit of hiccups. The comic bodily interruption reminds the reader that even exalted talk of love remains tied to a body that coughs, hiccups, and sneezes.

ann-053.title: The Physician's Way of Treating Hiccups
ann-053.content: Eryximachus immediately offers a medical remedy, mixing comedy with professional confidence. Bodily disorder and restored order preview the structure of his later speech.

ann-054.title: Love So Intense That Life Pauses
ann-054.content: Aristophanes describes the split halves clinging to one another so fiercely that they forget to eat or act. Love becomes the urge to rejoin oneself, even at the expense of ordinary life.

ann-055.title: Why Original Wholeness Was Lost
ann-055.content: The round humans were not innocently divided; they were punished for arrogant overreach against the gods. The myth therefore explains not only the search for another half but the moral background of that loss.

ann-056.title: Theater Applause Versus Rational Judgment
ann-056.content: Agathon distinguishes the crowd in the theater from the few who can judge well, bringing the problem of applause into the banquet itself. The line anticipates the coming contrast between what sounds good and what proves true.

ann-057.title: Phaedrus Protects the Agenda
ann-057.content: Phaedrus worries that if Socrates starts questioning too early, the whole evening will slide into argument. He acts here as more than a listener: he is the unofficial chair guarding the agreed format.

ann-058.title: Socrates Retreats Before He Strikes
ann-058.content: When he says there is little left for him to add, the remark sounds modest but prepares the ground for dialectic. Socrates often steps back rhetorically before reorganizing the conversation around questions.

ann-059.title: Diotima as an Outsider Authority
ann-059.content: Socrates assigns the dialogue's most important teaching on love to a wise woman from Mantinea. The authority to define eros is thus displaced away from the Athenian male speakers seated at the banquet.

ann-060.title: After Defining Love, Socrates Returns to Exhortation
ann-060.content: Once he finishes retelling Diotima, Socrates does not stop at abstract doctrine. He turns back toward advice about how we should honor Eros and learn the practice of love.

ann-061.title: Socrates Casts Himself as the Pursued One
ann-061.content: He jokes that Alcibiades will rage in jealousy, reversing the ordinary erotic script. Instead of Socrates chasing beauty, beauty seems to circle around Socrates.

ann-062.title: Even the Drunk Guest Must Obey the Rules
ann-062.content: Faced with the drunken entrance of Alcibiades, Eryximachus still tries to fold him back into the planned sequence. Symposium order collides head-on with intoxicated disruption.

ann-063.title: "Praise" Must Tell the Truth
ann-063.content: Socrates insists that if Alcibiades is going to praise him, he must speak truthfully. The demand shifts the speech away from mockery and toward risky testimony.

ann-064.title: Alcibiades Misreads Socrates Through Ordinary Desire
ann-064.content: He assumes Socrates must be drawn by youthful beauty like any other lover and plans to exchange bodily favor for wisdom. The mistake shows how thoroughly he still interprets Socrates through common erotic logic.

ann-065.title: The Trade of Body for Wisdom Fails
ann-065.content: Alcibiades imagines a profitable exchange: give beauty and intimacy, receive Socratic wisdom in return. Socrates' refusal rejects the idea that knowledge is a commodity that can be bartered.

ann-066.title: The Lover and the Beloved Reverse Places
ann-066.content: Alcibiades admits that Socrates behaves less like a pursuer than like someone pursued. The reversal both frustrates and fascinates him, exposing Socrates' mismatch with ordinary erotic roles.

ann-067.title: Socrates Immediately Sees the Jealous Strategy
ann-067.content: He identifies Alcibiades' real aim as driving a wedge between himself and Agathon. The speed of the diagnosis reframes drunken speech as a sharp contest of emotional strategy.

ann-068.title: The Middle Seat Becomes the Prize
ann-068.content: Alcibiades wants Agathon placed between them, turning seating arrangement into an emotional buffer and bargaining chip. Banquet space becomes a visible diagram of desire, rivalry, and embarrassment.

ann-069.title: A Spoken Performance on the Road into the City
ann-069.content: Glaucon asks Apollodorus to tell the whole story before they even reach the city. The opening of Symposium thus feels like an oral performance unfolding in shared motion.

ann-070.title: A 'Mad' Form of Devotion to Socrates
ann-070.content: Glaucon jokes that Apollodorus pities everyone except Socrates. The joke does more than tease: it frames Apollodorus as someone whose loyalty borders on religious intensity.

ann-071.title: Turning 'Madness' into Self-Reflection
ann-071.content: Apollodorus does not deny the charge head-on. Instead he reframes supposed madness as thinking about himself and his friends, redirecting mockery back toward philosophical self-examination.

ann-072.title: The Host's First Reflex Is Hospitality
ann-072.content: Agathon welcomes Aristodemus and immediately sends for Socrates. The gesture shows both the duties of a host and the special value Socrates already holds within the gathering.

ann-073.title: Light Drinking Is Not a Moral Lecture
ann-073.content: Aristophanes supports drinking lightly for a plain reason: everyone drank too much the night before. The resulting moderation feels bodily and practical rather than abstractly moralistic.

ann-074.title: Even the Host Stops Pretending to Be Hardy
ann-074.content: Agathon frankly admits he cannot keep drinking. As host he does not insist on heroic hospitality but allows the gathering to shift from competitive drinking to competitive speech.

ann-075.title: Even Sneezing Can Restore Order
ann-075.content: Aristophanes jokes that a harsh noise and a burst of discomfort can put the body back in order. The comic image unexpectedly fits Eryximachus's larger theme of harmony.

ann-076.title: The Pressure Is Passed to Agathon and Socrates
ann-076.content: Eryximachus praises Aristophanes for richness and variety, then lifts expectation onto Agathon and Socrates. The line formally hands the stage over to the dialogue's second half.

ann-077.title: Speaking Is Cast as Stepping onto a Stage
ann-077.content: Socrates describes his own coming speech as if it were an actor's entrance after others have already performed well. Banquet rhetoric and theatrical performance are made to mirror one another.

ann-078.title: The Allusion to Ate's Light Step
ann-078.content: Agathon borrows Homer's image of Ate stepping lightly to describe the delicacy of Eros. The point is less strict mythology than poetic atmosphere: love is rendered as soft, youthful, and almost weightless.

ann-079.title: Agathon Makes Love the Most Temperate Power
ann-079.content: Agathon claims that love rules desire and is untouched by violence, so it must be supremely temperate. Socrates will soon begin loosening this perfect portrait by asking what love still lacks.

ann-080.title: Desire Necessarily Points to Lack
ann-080.content: This exchange is one of the key links in Socrates' chain of reasoning: if desire reaches for what is not yet possessed, then love cannot already fully possess beauty and goodness.

ann-081.title: Exaggerated Courtesy in a Drunken Voice
ann-081.content: Alcibiades begins with inflated compliments and comic proverb-like banter toward Eryximachus. The style preserves the drama of drunken arrival while also signaling temporary submission to symposium procedure.

ann-082.title: The Chain of Retelling Includes a Small Verification
ann-082.content: Apollodorus says that he later checked a few details with Socrates himself. The memory is still layered and indirect, but it also makes a visible effort to claim a bit of reliability.

ann-083.title: Philosophy Is Embedded in Symposium Culture
ann-083.content: Libation, hymn, dinner, and drinking all belong to the Greek symposium setting. The dialogue's philosophy does not float free of social ritual but grows from within banquet culture.

ann-084.title: The Achilles Example Presumes Roles of Lover and Beloved
ann-084.content: Phaedrus insists that Achilles is younger and more beautiful, so he should count as the beloved rather than the lover. The claim reflects Greek assumptions about the roles of older pursuer and younger beloved.

ann-085.title: Pausanias Is Talking About Regulated Male Courtship
ann-085.content: These 'laws of love' mainly concern relations between adult men and youths under the pressure of custom and educational ideals. The passage is not a general theory of all private romance in a modern sense.

ann-086.title: Eryximachus Naturalizes Love
ann-086.content: In his hands, love is no longer only a human passion but a general principle of harmony in bodies, music, weather, and craft. Eros begins to take on the character of natural philosophy.

ann-087.title: The Myth Speaks in a Language of Healing
ann-087.content: Aristophanes describes finding one's companion as the healing of a split nature. His myth quietly reconnects with Eryximachus's medical vocabulary: love is both reunion and cure.

ann-088.title: This Is a Classic Encomium
ann-088.content: Agathon first defines how praise should proceed and then arranges his speech by moving from nature to benefits. The self-conscious structure is typical of classical encomiastic rhetoric.

ann-089.title: The Verbal Richness Is Part of the Argument
ann-089.content: Agathon's final movement piles up virtues, blessings, and balanced phrases not merely to sound beautiful. The speech uses verbal beauty itself to make Eros appear beautiful.

ann-090.title: The Plague Story Establishes Diotima's Authority
ann-090.content: When Socrates says that she helped delay the plague by ritual counsel, he is not tossing in trivia. He is establishing her authority in practical and religious terms before quoting her doctrine.

ann-091.title: Love Is Not Possessing Beauty but Giving Birth in It
ann-091.content: Diotima rewrites love's activity from acquiring a beautiful object to bringing forth in beauty. The shift is crucial because it turns love away from possession and toward generation.

ann-092.title: Contemplating Beauty Itself Gives Rise to True Virtue
ann-092.content: Diotima does not leave contemplation at the level of aesthetic pleasure. She says it can generate genuine virtue, reconnecting the metaphysical summit of the ascent with ethical life.

ann-093.title: Alcibiades as a Case of Incomplete Ascent
ann-093.content: He comes very close to Socrates and is deeply captivated by him, yet he still interprets the relationship mainly through honor, desire, and jealousy. In that sense he exemplifies someone who has not truly climbed the ladder of love.

ann-094.title: Socrates Returns to Ordinary Life After the Disorder
ann-094.content: While others collapse into sleep or confusion, Socrates leaves, bathes, and passes the day as usual. The ending quietly turns his restraint and endurance into lived proof.

ann-095.title: Socrates Turns Agathon's Own Method Back on Him
ann-095.content: He first agrees that Agathon is right to distinguish a thing's nature from its effects, then uses that very framework to ask what love is directed toward. The elenchus begins from inside Agathon's proudest definition, not from outside attack.

ann-096.title: "Beauty" and "Good" Are Brought Together Here
ann-096.content: Socrates gets Agathon to grant that the good is also beautiful, so that lacking beauty can be pushed further into lacking goodness. This move is crucial for Diotima's later account of love as desire for the good and for happiness.

ann-097.title: Right Opinion as an Intermediate State
ann-097.content: Diotima loosens the opposition between knowledge and ignorance by pointing to correct opinion. She uses this middle state to explain how love can stand between god and mortal, wisdom and foolishness.

ann-098.title: Only One Kind of Desire Gets Called 'Love'
ann-098.content: Diotima argues that everyone seeks the good and happiness in some way, but ordinary language reserves the word 'love' for only one especially heated form of that pursuit. Love is thus widened beyond narrow erotic attachment.

ann-099.title: Mortals Seek Immortality Through Renewal, Not Stasis
ann-099.content: Diotima says human beings cannot remain numerically identical the way gods can. They continue themselves through birth, memory, learning, and generational replacement, so immortality becomes ongoing renewal rather than changelessness.

ann-100.title: The Ladder Changes One's Vision, Not Just One's Object
ann-100.content: The ascent from one body to all bodies, then to institutions, knowledge, and Beauty itself is not merely a matter of loving more objects. It is a training of vision from possessive fixation toward universal understanding.

ann-101.title: True Virtue Versus What Only Looks Like Virtue
ann-101.content: Diotima distinguishes genuine virtue from things that merely resemble it. Virtue born after contemplation of Beauty itself is no longer just ambition, imitation, or social display.

ann-102.title: Philosophical Content Still Wears the Shell of Encomium
ann-102.content: After retelling Diotima, Socrates still says the speech may be called a praise of Eros if one likes. He does not simply abandon the banquet genre; he transforms encomium into philosophy.

ann-103.title: Alcibiades Turns Abstract Doctrine into Lived Experience
ann-103.content: Diotima explained love and ascent conceptually; Alcibiades shows, from experience, why Socrates attracts, unsettles, and shames those around him. The abstract account receives an emotional confirmation here.

ann-104.title: The Final Survivors Represent Three Genres
ann-104.content: At dawn the ones still awake are Aristophanes, Agathon, and Socrates, corresponding to comedy, tragedy, and philosophy. The last argument about whether one person can write both comedy and tragedy reads like the dialogue's own self-summary.

ann-105.title: Love Is First of All a Relational Term
ann-105.content: Socrates begins with examples like father and brother to show that love cannot be treated as a self-standing thing. It is always love of something, a relation directed toward an object.

ann-106.title: Socrates Puts Himself Back in the Place of a Student
ann-106.content: After dismantling Agathon's speech, Socrates does not present himself as the triumphant master. He says that he too once received instruction from Diotima, returning the strongest speaker to the position of a learner.

ann-107.title: Philosophy Is Born in the Middle
ann-107.content: The gods do not seek wisdom because they already possess it, and the purely ignorant do not seek it because they do not even feel the lack. The desire for wisdom belongs to those who stand between the two.

ann-108.title: Animals Are Also Drawn into Love's Logic
ann-108.content: Diotima turns to animals nursing offspring and fighting for their young in order to show that the pursuit of immortality is not a uniquely human refinement. It is a more general impulse of living things.

ann-109.title: "The Same Person" Is Constantly Being Renewed
ann-109.content: Body, character, knowledge, and memory are always being replaced, so the individual is not an unchanging identity. Mortals participate in permanence only through continuous renewal.

ann-110.title: The Final Revelation Arrives Suddenly
ann-110.content: The ladder of love is described as a gradual discipline up to the top, but the highest vision appears all at once. Plato uses that rhythm to portray philosophy as a sudden illumination after long preparation.

ann-111.title: Being a 'Friend of the Gods' Does Not Mean Becoming a God
ann-111.content: The contemplator of Beauty itself is called dear to the gods because he shares most fully in immortality, not because he ceases to be human. The dialogue preserves the distance between mortals and gods even at its highest point.

ann-112.title: Socrates Still Aims to Persuade Others
ann-112.content: He says he not only accepts Diotima's teaching but wants others to receive it as well. Philosophy here is not private vision alone but a practice of public persuasion.

ann-113.title: Alcibiades Also Speaks in the Language of Initiation
ann-113.content: He says that Socrates' words reveal who is fit for sacred mysteries. That phrasing echoes Diotima's earlier language of initiation, ascent, and final revelation.

ann-114.title: The Witness Falls Asleep and Leaves a Final Gap
ann-114.content: Aristodemus sleeps at the crucial moment and wakes only half-aware, so the final poetic debate is not fully preserved. The ending keeps a deliberate residue of incompleteness.

characterEnglishBioById.apollodorus: Apollodorus of Phalerum was an ancient Athenian and one of Socrates' followers. At the opening of Symposium, he carries the outermost layer of recollection and retelling.
characterEnglishBioById.glaucon: Glaucon was an Athenian aristocrat and one of Plato's brothers. In several Platonic dialogues he appears as one of Socrates' important interlocutors.
characterEnglishBioById.aristodemus: Aristodemus of Cydathenaeum was an ancient Athenian and one of Socrates' close companions. In Symposium, he is written as the eyewitness who attended the banquet and saw the whole sequence unfold.
characterEnglishBioById.phaedrus: Phaedrus was an Athenian aristocrat and a recurrent figure in Plato's dialogues. In Symposium, he is the first to propose that each guest praise Eros in turn.
characterEnglishBioById.pausanias: Pausanias was an Athenian aristocrat closely associated with the tragedian Agathon. In Symposium, he is known for distinguishing heavenly love from common love.
characterEnglishBioById.eryximachus: Eryximachus was an Athenian physician. Plato portrays him as the rational speaker who extends love into the body, music, and cosmic order.
characterEnglishBioById.aristophanes: Aristophanes was one of the most important comic poets of ancient Greece, known for plays such as Clouds, Birds, and Frogs. In Symposium, the famous myth of the round humans comes from his speech.
characterEnglishBioById.agathon: Agathon was an Athenian tragic poet celebrated for ornate style. The banquet of Symposium takes place after his victory in a dramatic competition.
characterEnglishBioById.socrates: Socrates was an ancient Greek philosopher and a foundational source for Western ethics and dialectic. The intellectual center of Symposium unfolds through his retelling of Diotima's account of love.
characterEnglishBioById.diotima: Diotima is the Mantinean wise woman or priestly figure introduced by Socrates in Symposium. Later readers often treat her as a crucial source for the idea of 'Platonic love.'
characterEnglishBioById.alcibiades: Alcibiades was a famous Athenian statesman, general, and orator, known for brilliance, ambition, and a volatile political career. At the end of Symposium he bursts into the banquet and offers a personal testimony about Socrates.
characterEnglishBioById.eros: Eros is the Greek god of love and, in classical tradition, a figure for desire, attraction, and generative force. The entire discussion of Symposium revolves around him.
characterEnglishBioById.aphrodite: Aphrodite is the Greek goddess of love and beauty, often associated with desire, charm, and fertility. Pausanias uses her doubled genealogy to distinguish different ranks of love.

characterEnglishSummaryById.apollodorus: On the road into the city, he retells the whole banquet at Glaucon's request and provides the outermost frame of memory in Symposium.
characterEnglishSummaryById.glaucon: He stops Apollodorus outside the city and asks him to retell the conversation about love that took place at Agathon's house.
characterEnglishSummaryById.aristodemus: He actually attends the banquet and witnesses it from beginning to end, serving as Apollodorus's main source.
characterEnglishSummaryById.phaedrus: He proposes that each guest praise Eros in turn, opening the discussion through honor, courage, and sacrifice.
characterEnglishSummaryById.pausanias: He distinguishes heavenly love from common love and steers the discussion toward virtue and civic ethics.
characterEnglishSummaryById.eryximachus: He extends love into the body, music, the seasons, and ritual, emphasizing order, restraint, and harmony.
characterEnglishSummaryById.aristophanes: He explains human lack and the desire for companionship through the myth of the round humans, offering the dialogue's most famous poetic image.
characterEnglishSummaryById.agathon: He praises Eros through elegant rhetoric, celebrating youth, softness, and happiness before submitting to Socrates' questions.
characterEnglishSummaryById.socrates: Through cross-examination he dismantles the hymns of praise and, by retelling Diotima's teaching, turns the discussion into philosophical definition.
characterEnglishSummaryById.diotima: She presents love as a daimon between lack and resource and lays out the ascending path known as the ladder of love.
characterEnglishSummaryById.alcibiades: Through personal experience he sketches Socrates' restraint, courage, and spiritual force, giving the whole dialogue a testimonial portrait.
characterEnglishSummaryById.eros: The shared object of the entire dialogue: described as a source of courage, a principle of harmony, and a seeker working from lack.
characterEnglishSummaryById.aphrodite: Pausanias uses the two Aphrodites to distinguish heavenly love from common love and establish an ethical hierarchy.

placeEnglishSummaryById.phalerum: The harbor district from which Apollodorus walks into the city, and the real-world setting from which the retelling begins.
placeEnglishSummaryById.athens: The political and cultural background of the whole dialogue; debates about law, virtue, rhetoric, and philosophy all take Athens as their reference point.
placeEnglishSummaryById.agathon-house: The main setting of the banquet, where the praises of Eros, Socrates' cross-examination, and Alcibiades' drunken encomium all unfold.
placeEnglishSummaryById.cydathenaeum: The Athenian district to which Aristodemus belongs, specifying the concrete identity of the main eyewitness.
placeEnglishSummaryById.mantinea: Diotima's home, through which Socrates introduces another lineage of wisdom in order to redefine love.
placeEnglishSummaryById.potidaea: The battlefield where Alcibiades recalls campaigning with Socrates, used to show Socrates' endurance and courage.
placeEnglishSummaryById.delium: Another battlefield in the retreat narrative, highlighting Socrates' composure and self-command in danger.
placeEnglishSummaryById.lyceum: The place where Socrates bathes and spends the day as usual after the banquet, showing how little the drinking party disturbs his routine.

relationEdgeEnglishById.edge-001: Asks for the banquet to be retold
relationEdgeEnglishById.edge-002: Hears the banquet story from him
relationEdgeEnglishById.edge-003: Follows him and witnesses his arrival and speech
relationEdgeEnglishById.edge-005: Uses the double Aphrodite to distinguish two loves
relationEdgeEnglishById.edge-006: Extends love into medicine and cosmic harmony
relationEdgeEnglishById.edge-007: Explains love through the myth of the round humans
relationEdgeEnglishById.edge-008: Celebrates Eros in a rhetorical encomium
relationEdgeEnglishById.edge-011: Defines love as a daimon between lack and resource
relationEdgeEnglishById.edge-012: Uses personal experience to depict his virtue and charm

relationNodeEnglishSummaryById.glaucon: Asks Apollodorus to retell the banquet.
relationNodeEnglishSummaryById.phaedrus: Raises the theme of praising Eros.
relationNodeEnglishSummaryById.eryximachus: Explains love through medicine and harmony.
relationNodeEnglishSummaryById.aristophanes: Uses myth to describe human division.
relationNodeEnglishSummaryById.agathon: Host of the banquet and celebrant of rhetorical praise.
relationNodeEnglishSummaryById.eros: The thematic center around which all speeches turn.
relationNodeEnglishSummaryById.aphrodite: The key deity in the distinction between two loves.

themeEnglishNameById.theme-wholeness: Divided Humans and the Search for the Other Half

themeEnglishSummaryById.theme-frame: Symposium is not a direct transcript but a layered retelling, passing through Aristodemus and Apollodorus before reaching Glaucon.
themeEnglishSummaryById.theme-honor: Phaedrus presents love as a source of shame, sacrifice, and courage in battle.
themeEnglishSummaryById.theme-two-loves: Pausanias uses the double Aphrodite to distinguish higher and lower forms of love, bringing love under law and virtue.
themeEnglishSummaryById.theme-cosmic-order: Eryximachus extends love into medicine, music, the seasons, and the traffic between gods and mortals.
themeEnglishSummaryById.theme-wholeness: Aristophanes uses the myth of the round humans to depict human lack and interpret love as the search for wholeness.
themeEnglishSummaryById.theme-ladder: Diotima understands love as the desire for the good and for immortality, culminating in contemplation of Beauty itself.
themeEnglishSummaryById.theme-socratic-portrait: Alcibiades turns the abstract philosophy of love into a portrait of Socrates' restraint, shame, and battlefield virtue.
